items.html: Identify anti-oppressive, anti-racist, strengths-based, and trauma-informed practices
Discuss need for safer health care spaces for racialized patients and families
As healthcare workers, we operate in a society that is characterized by power imbalances within systems, including healthcare, education, housing, employment, immigration, and justice.
Power is dispersed within society based on social identities related to race, age, immigration status, gender, sexual orientation, ability, geographical location, income, and more.
An Anti-Oppressive framework recognizes how a person's personal experiences are linked to and shaped by oppressive systems.

data.html: According to Ontario Health's Sickle Cell Disease Quality Standard, SCD disproportionately impacts Black communities. Anti-Black Racism profoundly shapes the health and wellbeing of people with SCD.

Anti-Black Racism: The Black Health Alliance defines Anti-Black Racism as “policies and practices rooted in Canadian institutions such as, education, health care, and justice that mirror and reinforce beliefs, attitudes, prejudice, stereotyping and/or discrimination towards people of Black-African Descent.”

'Anti-Black Racism' was first developed by Dr. Akua Benjamin, a professor emeritus from Toronto Metropolitan University's Social Work program. The concept speaks to the systemic racism faced by Black-Canadians as well as the impacts of enslavement and colonization for Black and African descent peoples.

images.caption: Poverty and Sickle Cell Disease — Poverty refers to a lack of access to meet the basic needs of life, including shelter, food, and clothing. Poverty is a result of systemic inequities in the economy and society. These are due to a lack of work, low wages, and discrimination — factors beyond the control of individuals. 41% of all peoples with SCD in Ontario live in neighbourhoods with the lowest incomes.

For people with Sickle Cell Disease, who are already dealing with uncertainty about their health, poverty creates additional stress that exacerbates overall mental health and wellbeing. Resource: The RBCD Hub’s article, Down with Capitalism, explores how people with sickle cell disease are particularly vulnerable in direct and indirect ways under our capitalist economy.

items.title: Non-accommodation and disability
items.html: Non-accommodation and disability-related insults can lead to stress and anxiety. It can also create barriers to accessing much needed resources.

items.title: Sickle cell as invisible disability
items.html: Sickle cell disease can be an invisible disability. People have their own definitions of disability. Identifying with disability is up to the person.

Remember: Doctors document and adjudicators judicate!

data.html:
What percent of individuals with SCD live in the lowest income neighbourhoods? (Round answer)

Answer: 41%

41% of all peoples with SCD in Ontario live in neighbourhoods with the lowest incomes. Poverty is a result of systemic inequities in the economy and society. These are due to a lack of work, low wages, and discrimination — factors beyond the control of individuals.

slides.title: In Their Own Words: Black woman living with sickle cell disease faces discrimination

items.html: Dr. Kimberlé Crenshaw developed the concept of intersectionality to understand how social identities related to race, gender, disability, class, and so forth interact with one another.
Intersectionality directs attention to how different systems of oppression, including racism, sexism, ableism, classism, and others, interlock with one another to shape people's experiences.

People with SCD are not a monolithic group — they have diverse identities and are navigating multiple worlds. For healthcare workers, understanding and responding to intersectionality is imperative for supporting mental health and wellbeing.

data.html: Resource: The RBCD Clinic’s session on Intersectionality and Mental Health explores how racism and anti-Black racism, disability, and other social identities intersect and shape mental health and wellbeing for people with sickle cell disease and other red blood cell disorders: https://rbcd.ca/blog/intersectionality-and-mental-health

slides.title: Key Terms For Health Equity for Individuals with SCD

data.html:
Drag the letters to spell words related to Anti-Racism and Anti-Oppressive Healthcare for individuals with SCD.

Key terms: Intersectionality, Disability invisibility, Trauma informed care, Anti Black Racism

items.html: Anti-oppressive, anti-racist, strengths-based, and trauma-informed approaches can help provide safer care
Informed and patient-centered clinicians have a role to play to support mental health and connections to resources for patients with sickle cell disease
Systems, including that of healthcare, education, housing, employment, immigration, and justice, have been harmful to people with sickle cell disease
